dimensions.label: Research craft
dimensions.description: Comfort designing studies, recruiting, and analyzing qualitative + mixed data.

dimensions.label: Pace & ambiguity
dimensions.description: Tolerance for 2-week timelines and decisions made on partial evidence.

dimensions.label: Product sense
dimensions.description: Ability to connect research to design, engineering, and business decisions.

dimensions.label: Communication
dimensions.description: Writing tight readouts and presenting to non-researchers.

dimensions.label: Stakeholder fluency
dimensions.description: Working with product managers, designers, and executives, and saying no without burning bridges.

questions.prompt: I can plan a small interview study (about 8 people) and have a written summary within two weeks.
I'm comfortable watching someone use software and asking them to narrate what they're thinking.
I can write a one-page research plan with clear questions, methods, and the decisions it will inform.
I can analyze a mix of qualitative and quantitative data and resist over-interpreting small samples.
I'm comfortable acting on an 80%-confident finding when the decision can be reversed later.
I can change my study mid-way when the question I'm trying to answer shifts.
I'd rather deliver a useful answer in two weeks than a perfect one in two months.
I can explain how a research finding should change what gets built, measured, or shown on screen.
I can read software documents, design mockups, and usage dashboards without getting lost.
I can recognize when research isn't the right tool and suggest a cheaper way to get the answer.
I think about the specific person trying to get something done — not just populations and average effects.
I can write a one-page summary that a busy non-academic colleague will actually read.
I'm comfortable presenting findings to skeptical engineers or executives.
I can replace academic hedging with plain claims while still being honest about uncertainty.
I can disagree with a designer or product manager and still get invited to the next meeting.
I can turn down a research request and offer a useful alternative.
I can read the politics of a room and adjust how I deliver bad news.
I'm energized — not drained — by working with non-researchers every day.

bands.label: Not yet
bands.blurb: You have real research instincts, but the pace and politics of this work will frustrate you today. Use the next six months to ship one small applied study.

bands.label: Curious
bands.blurb: You'd survive a UX research role with deliberate practice. Focus on speed, product framing, and short summaries.

bands.label: Strong fit
bands.blurb: Your research craft transfers cleanly. Spend your effort on portfolio examples and stakeholder stories.

bands.label: Ready to apply
bands.blurb: On paper you're already a UX researcher. The remaining gap is positioning — your CV, work samples, and interview stories.
